summary: Design a multi-class classification and interpretability pipeline that distinguishes rheumatoid arthritis (RA), psoriatic arthritis (PsA), and osteoarthritis (OA) from hand/feet X-ray images. The architecture combines a CNN backbone (e.g., DenseNet or EfficientNet) for disease classification with an attention-based or Grad-CAM explanation module to highlight radiographically relevant joint regions, addressing both discrimination and clinical interpretability.
assumptions: Available dataset of anonymized hand/feet X-ray images with per-image disease labels (RA, PsA, OA, healthy) from the Department of Rheumatology, with sufficient sample size per class for supervised learning.
Images are preprocessed to a standardized resolution and orientation (e.g., center-cropped to a fixed size, histogram equalization for contrast normalization across scanner variations).
Compute environment with at least one GPU (e.g., NVIDIA RTX 3090 or A100) and standard Python ML stack (PyTorch, MONAI/torchvision, NumPy).
Radiographic ground-truth labels are adjudicated by a trained rheumatologist (Jana Eder / Peter Mandl) to ensure label quality, with inter-reader agreement considered for a subset.
questions: What is the granularity of available labels — per-image disease class only, or also per-joint structural annotations (e.g., erosion scores, JSN grades, bounding boxes)? This drives whether the architecture needs segmentation/detection heads beyond classification.

Is the dataset limited to a single anatomical view (e.g., bilateral hand PA) or does it include multiple views/sites (hands, feet, knees)? Multi-site data would require a multi-input or Siamese-style fusion design.
